title_en: The Proofs and Evidence for the Three Principles
content_id:
Ciri khas dari Al-Usul ath-Thalathah adalah penekanannya pada bukti. Ibn Abdul Wahhab tidak hanya menyatakan apa yang harus diyakini oleh kaum Muslim — beliau secara konsisten bertanya 'Apa dalilnya?' (Ma al-dalil?) dan memberikan jawabannya dari Alquran dan Sunnah yang sahih. Bab ini mengkonsolidasikan dan menjelaskan metodologi bukti yang beliau gunakan di seluruh risalah.

Pengarang membedakan antara dua jenis pengetahuan: taklid (peniruan buta tanpa memahami dalil) dan ittiba (mengikuti berdasarkan pemahaman bukti). Sementara taklid memiliki tempatnya dalam masalah-masalah hukum yang bersifat sekunder bagi orang awam, dalam masalah-masalah aqidah yang bersifat fundamental, taklid saja tidak cukup. Setiap Muslim harus mengetahui tidak hanya apa yang harus diyakini tetapi mengapa — berakar pada wahyu, bukan sekadar tradisi atau kebiasaan budaya.

Untuk prinsip pertama — pengetahuan tentang Allah — bukti primernya adalah surah pembuka Alquran itu sendiri. Al-Fatihah, yang dibaca dalam setiap rakaat shalat, dimulai dengan: 'Segala puji milik Allah, Tuhan semesta alam.' Frasa 'Tuhan semesta alam' (Rabb al-alamin) menetapkan ketuhanan ilahi atas setiap makhluk ciptaan. Bukti lebih lanjut datang dari Ayat al-Kursi (Al-Baqarah, 2:255), yang Nabi gambarkan sebagai ayat terbesar dalam Alquran. Ayat ini merinci sifat-sifat kedaulatan, pengetahuan, dan kekuatan pemeliharaan Allah dengan cara yang tidak tertandingi oleh ayat lain mana pun.

Untuk keesaan ibadah secara khusus, Ibn Abdul Wahhab berulang kali kembali kepada Surah Al-Kafirun ('Katakanlah: Wahai orang-orang kafir...') dan Surah Al-Ikhlas ('Katakanlah: Dia-lah Allah, Yang Maha Esa...'). Surah-surah pendek ini merangkum seluruh doktrin tauhid al-uluhiyyah. Nabi bersabda bahwa Surah Al-Ikhlas setara pahala dengan sepertiga Alquran karena deskripsinya yang lengkap tentang keesaan unik Allah (Sahih Bukhari dan Muslim).

Untuk prinsip kedua — pengetahuan tentang Islam — lanskap dalil-dalilnya sangat luas. Lima pilar masing-masing ditetapkan oleh perintah-perintah Alquran yang jelas yang dipasangkan dengan riwayat kenabian. Hadis Jibril (Sahih Muslim) barangkali adalah hadis paling penting untuk bab ini, karena merangkum Islam, iman, dan ihsan dalam satu pertukaran antara Nabi dan malaikat yang datang dalam wujud manusia. Nabi menutup riwayat itu dengan mengatakan: 'Itu adalah Jibril yang datang untuk mengajari kamu agamamu.'

Untuk prinsip ketiga — pengetahuan tentang Nabi — dalil kenabiannya adalah Alquran: 'Muhammad itu bukan bapak dari seseorang di antara kamu, tetapi ia adalah utusan Allah dan penutup para nabi' (Al-Ahzab, 33:40). Kata-kata Nabi sendiri dalam hadis tentang kenabian (Sahih Bukhari) — 'Aku diutus kepada semua manusia merah dan hitam' — lebih lanjut menetapkan ruang lingkup universal misinya.

Ibn Abdul Wahhab juga membahas mengapa bukti penting dalam praktik. Beliau mengutip adegan kubur secara langsung: orang yang meninggal ditanya tiga pertanyaan, dan mereka yang menjawab dengan benar adalah mereka yang mempelajari prinsip-prinsip ini beserta dalil-dalilnya selama hidup mereka. Nabi bersabda: 'Ketika seorang mukmin diinterogasi di dalam kuburnya, ia akan bersaksi bahwa tidak ada tuhan yang berhak disembah selain Allah dan bahwa Muhammad adalah utusan Allah — itulah makna firman Allah: 'Allah mengukuhkan orang-orang yang beriman dengan ucapan yang teguh dalam kehidupan dunia dan di akhirat'' (Lihat Ibrahim, 14:27; diriwayatkan dalam Sahih Bukhari dan Muslim).

Bab ini ditutup dengan pengingat bahwa pengetahuan berbasis bukti menghasilkan keyakinan yang teguh dan tidak tergoyahkan — tidak hanya pada saat kematian, tetapi dalam kehidupan sehari-hari, ketika keraguan-keraguan muncul, ketika ujian-ujian menekan, dan ketika seorang mukmin harus berdiri teguh dalam dunia yang terus-menerus menantang fundamental-fundamental keimanan.
content_en:
A defining feature of Al-Usul ath-Thalathah is its insistence on evidence. Ibn Abdul Wahhab does not simply state what Muslims must believe — he consistently asks "What is the proof?" (Ma al-dalil?) and supplies the answer from the Quran and authentic Sunnah. This chapter consolidates and explains the methodology of evidence he employs throughout the treatise.

The author distinguishes between two types of knowledge: taqlid (blind imitation without understanding the proof) and ittiba' (following based on understanding the evidence). While taqlid has its place in subsidiary legal matters for the layperson, in matters of foundational creed it is not sufficient. Every Muslim must know not only what to believe but why — grounded in revelation, not merely tradition or cultural habit.

For the first principle — knowledge of Allah — the primary proof is the opening chapter of the Quran itself. Al-Fatiha, recited in every rak'ah of prayer, begins with: "All praise is due to Allah, the Lord of all the worlds." The phrase "Lord of all the worlds" (Rabb al-'alamin) establishes divine lordship over every created thing. Further proof comes from the Verse of the Footstool (Ayat al-Kursi, Al-Baqarah, 2:255), which the Prophet described as the greatest verse in the Quran. It enumerates the attributes of Allah's sovereignty, knowledge, and sustaining power in a manner unmatched by any other verse.

For the oneness of worship specifically, Ibn Abdul Wahhab returns repeatedly to Surah Al-Kafirun ("Say: O disbelievers...") and Surah Al-Ikhlas ("Say: He is Allah, the One..."). These short surahs encapsulate the entire doctrine of tawhid al-uluhiyyah. The Prophet said that Surah Al-Ikhlas is equivalent in reward to one-third of the Quran because of its complete description of Allah's unique oneness (Sahih Bukhari and Muslim).

For the second principle — knowledge of Islam — the proof landscape is broad. The five pillars are each established by clear Quranic commands paired with prophetic narration. The hadith of Jibril (Sahih Muslim) is perhaps the single most important hadith for this chapter, as it encapsulates Islam, iman, and ihsan in one exchange between the Prophet and the angel sent in human form. The Prophet concluded the narration by saying: "That was Jibril who came to teach you your religion."

For the third principle — knowledge of the Prophet — the proof of his prophethood is the Quran: "Muhammad is not the father of any of your men, but he is the Messenger of Allah and the seal of the prophets" (Al-Ahzab, 33:40). The Prophet's own words in the hadith of prophethood (Sahih al-Bukhari) — "I was sent to all of mankind in red and black" — further establish the universal scope of his mission.

Ibn Abdul Wahhab also addresses the question of why evidence matters in practice. He cites the scene of the grave directly: the dead person is asked three questions, and those who answer correctly are those who learned these principles with their proofs during their lives. The Prophet said: "When the believer is questioned in the grave, he will testify that there is no deity worthy of worship but Allah and that Muhammad is the Messenger of Allah — that is the meaning of Allah's statement: 'Allah keeps firm those who believe with a firm word in worldly life and in the Hereafter'" (Al-Baqarah, 2:186 — see Ibrahim, 14:27; narrated in Sahih Bukhari and Muslim).

The chapter concludes with a reminder that evidence-based knowledge produces firm, unshakeable conviction — not just at the hour of death, but in daily life, when doubts are raised, when trials press in, and when the believer must stand firm in a world that constantly challenges the fundamentals of the faith.
content_ar: A defining feature of Al-الأصول ath-Thalathah is its insistence on evidence. Ibn Abdul Wahhab does not simply state what المسلمون must believe — he consistently asks "What is the proof?" (Ma al-dalil?) and supplies the answer from the القرآن الكريم and authentic السنة. This الباب consolidates and explains the methodology of evidence he employs throughout the treatise. The author distinguishes between two types of العلم: taqlid (blind imitation without الفهم the proof) and ittiba' (following based on الفهم the evidence). بينما taqlid has its place in subsidiary legal matters for the layperson, in matters of foundational creed it is not sufficient. Every مسلم must know not only what to believe but why — grounded in الوحي, not merely التراث or cultural habit. For the أولاً principle — العلم of الله — the الأساسي proof is the opening الباب of the القرآن الكريم itself. Al-Fatiha, recited in every rak'ah of الصلاة, begins with: "All praise is بسبب الله, the Lord of all the worlds." The phrase "Lord of all the worlds" (Rabb al-'alamin) establishes divine lordship over every created thing. Further proof comes from the الآية of the Footstool (Ayat al-Kursi, Al-Baqarah, 2:255), which النبي ﷺ described as the greatest الآية in the القرآن الكريم. It enumerates the attributes of الله's sovereignty, العلم, and sustaining power in a manner unmatched by any other الآية. For the oneness of worship specifically, Ibn Abdul Wahhab returns repeatedly to السورة Al-Kafirun ("Say: O disbelievers...") and السورة Al-Ikhlas ("Say: He is الله, the One..."). These short السور encapsulate the entire doctrine of tawhid al-uluhiyyah. النبي ﷺ said that السورة Al-Ikhlas is equivalent in الأجر to one-ثالثاً of the القرآن الكريم لأن of its complete description of الله's unique oneness (صحيح البخاري and مسلم). For the ثانياً principle — العلم of الإسلام — the proof landscape is broad. The five pillars are each established by clear القرآني commands paired with prophetic narration. The الحديث of جبريل ﷺ (صحيح مسلم) is perhaps the single most المهم الحديث for this الباب, as it encapsulates الإسلام, الإيمان, and ihsan in one exchange between النبي ﷺ and the angel sent in human form. النبي ﷺ concluded the narration by saying: "That was جبريل ﷺ who came to teach you your religion." For the ثالثاً principle — العلم of النبي ﷺ — the proof of his prophethood is the القرآن الكريم: "محمد ﷺ is not the الأب of any of your men, but he is الرسول ﷺ of الله and the seal of the الأنبياء" (Al-Ahzab, 33:40). النبي ﷺ's own words in the الحديث of prophethood (صحيح البخاري) — "I was sent to all of mankind in red and black" — further establish the universal scope of his mission. Ibn Abdul Wahhab أيضاً addresses the question of why evidence matters in practice.
